title: Stay Up OG
body:
Outside the Acme,
a man sat on the curb,
his face half-shadow, half-story.

I asked if he was hungry—
he said yeah, real quick,
like he'd been waiting all day
for someone to ask that question.

I asked if he had any health issues,
because I was thinking about
the family eight-piece fried chicken—
enough to last a couple days maybe.

He looked at me and said,
get me a Diet Coke too,
like it wasn't a question.

Something in his tone
barked instead of spoke,
and I felt it,
that small ache that comes
when your kindness isn't met
with softness.

Still, I went inside.
Got the chicken.
Two Diet Pepsis—
they didn't have Coke.

Came back out,
held the bag out like an offering.
He took it,
no thank you,
no nod,
just an aight.

And I won't lie,
it bothered me.
Because I wanted it to feel good.
I wanted him to see I cared.

But maybe saying thank you
was harder for him
than asking for food.

My mom always said,
One day, it might be you.
Help if you can.

So I did.

And maybe that's what kindness is—
doing it anyway,
even when no one claps,
even when the world
doesn't bow its head and say thanks.

I told him, Stay up, OG.
He said, aight.

And somehow,
that was enough.
